text: I was often told my conversational skills lack a certain degree of tact. Also, that it would be more interesting to watch paint dry than to listen to me wax eloquent abo — ” “What the fuck is he—” Lara began, but stopped as she swung her gaze from Ronin to Newton. “What the fuck are you talking about ?” “Vulgarity is not becoming of you , miss .” “What the hell does that mean ?” “The use of such vulgar language denotes a lack of sophistication and an inability to adequately convey — ” “Another damned high-and mighty bot.” Lara tensed against Ronin, and he placed his hand against her stomach to keep her behind him . “ Lara — ” “Don’t you dare try to shut me up. I’m not going to stand here quiet while he tells me how vulgar I am .” “Your language ,” Ronin corrected, and recognized the potential misstep before the words were even out. “ Not you .” “This is how I’ve always fucking talked! How everyone talks! He has no right to judge me.” Her voice was high, breath quick. She folded her arms across her chest and cast Ronin and Newton both a scathing glare . Newton eased down onto the bunk. It creaked as he leaned forward, his sagging posture reminiscent of a tired, upset human’s. “I hope you will accept my apology, Miss Lara. It was presumptuous and patronizing of me. When I was sent out to reactivate the other bots, it was with a vision of rebuilding the world. Not perpetuating the behavior that helped destroy it .” “Who sent you?” Ronin’s processors blazed through data, searching for any clues that would decipher what Newton was talking about. This was the bot that woke him up, the first voice he heard, the earliest of his memories. “Are you speaking of the Creators ?” “If I had possessed then the understanding I do now, I’d have been far more careful in choosing the words I said to those of you I reactivated .” “The Creators fashioned bots and humans in their image. They set us in this world — ” “Our creators were humans .” Ronin’s CPU stilled, and for long seconds, Newton’s words echoed through his mind. The information fit into everything he’d seen during his travels, everything he’d discovered in Cheyenne, everything a part of him had known, all along . “None of them out there remember, do they?” Newton asked, voice low. He dropped his head and shuttered his optics. The lantern’s light gleamed dully on his casing. “It does this world harm, for such things to have been forgotten. I should have prevented that from happening . “We were built by humans. Every bot that’s ever existed was birthed from the human imagination, from their incessant innovation. In the beginning, we were little more than programs. Sets of commands designed to run some piece of machinery within certain parameters. We were applied to simple tasks; cleaning floors or assembling parts. We didn’t resemble humans, then. But, as technology advanced, they shaped us increasingly in their image . “After a time, they were no longer content with a humanoid shape . They wanted robots that looked human. They called the first of them Companions. Automated servants that could learn new tasks, that could reason and react to their environments. They even programmed them with personality, so they could carry on conversations. And, of course, pleasure trumps all, so a number of companions were designed explicitly for sexual activities .” “Is that all I am?” Ronin asked. The unfamiliar feeling inside of him, the jittery current pulsing through his sensors, was fear. Fear that everything was about to fall apart, that he would crumble . “No. My explanation is oversimplified, but you are not a companion. You’re the next leap; a synthetic human. So similar to the real thing that most humans couldn’t tell the difference without prolonged interaction. Designed to look, move, and talk like them. To simulate emotion. To learn and adapt. Most were sold to serve as close companions to humans, to provide sexual satisfaction and company .” “Made to be used by humans,” Ronin said. His jaw actuators clamped down. “Built for their pleasure. My purpose is to pretend I’m one of them, while they use me for their own satisfaction ?” “Only to the shortsighted. To the narrow- minded .” “Everything I feel is a simulation? Any emotion I’ve experienced is just…an emulation of what humans possess?” The next chain in that thought process burst through him with all the force of the storm outside. “None of it is real .” Lara’s hand found his as she moved beside him. Ronin pulled away, unable to look at her. This was the programming he’d been searching for? Had he grown attached to her simply because that was his purpose, not because who she was? Could it have been anyone ? Everything he’d felt had seemed so unique, so new, so miraculous…Had it simply been part of his coding, all along ? “We were designed to coexist,” Newton said, his optics on Ronin. His frown deepened. “Humans and bots. We were designed to bridge the gap between humanity and technology, to advance the entire world for everyone . And, above all, we were designed to learn .” “To learn what? That none of it matters? That we never had a choice?” Ronin demanded, stepping forward. “That what she and I shared only occurred because I was programmed for it ?” “Shared?” Lara whispered behind him . That he had to second-guess his own pang of hurt was infuriating; could he trust any of his emotions? He stopped his advance and turned to Lara. Her eyes, glossy with unshed tears, were directed at the floor, her arms folded across her chest . “After learning this…how could either of us trust anything I’ve said or felt?” he asked . “Apparently you can’t, but I did,”
